content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 29, 2002

Dan Lee
Treasurer for Councilmember Rob Schroder
1433 Mellissa Circle
Antioch, California 94509

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-116

Dear Mr. Lee:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Rob Schroder regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Must Councilmember Schroder report contributions received and expenditures made to mail out a questionnaire even though no ballot measure is being advocated by the questionnaire?

2. Will it make a difference if Councilmember Schroder mails questionnaires only to the 2,600 constituents who responded to a previous questionnaire sent out by the council member?

3. If Councilmember Schroder stops production on the mail piece and gives someone else the notes, drafts, and outlines for the purpose of sending a similar questionnaire, will Councilmember Schroder incur any reporting obligations?

CONCLUSIONS

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, sections 18009-18997. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-116
Page No. 2

1. Councilmember Schroder must report all contributions received and expenditures made for political purposes including those made for the purpose of mailing out the questionnaire.

2. This conclusion will not change even if Councilmember Schroder restricts the mailing to the 2,600 constituents who responded to a previous questionnaire sent out by Councilmember Schroder.

3. If another individual or entity mails the questionnaire at Councilmember Schroder's behest, Councilmember Schroder will incur reporting obligations if the entity makes a payment of $5,000 or more in a calendar year to send the mailing.

FACTS

You are treasurer for Martinez City Councilmember Rob Schroder, a current City of Martinez council member who is running for the position of mayor on the November 2002 ballot. Councilmember Schroder wishes to send out a cover letter and questionnaire regarding the Martinez Downtown and Waterfront Revitalization project and asking whether voters:

▪ Generally support or oppose revitalizing the downtown and waterfront area;

▪ Would be more or less likely to support revitalization knowing that up to 300 new homes a year, for ten years, might be built in the area now used for industrial storage,

▪ Would be more or less likely to support revitalization knowing that development fees would pay for the building of a new downtown elementary school as well as additional parks, recreational areas, additional open space and more marshland protection;

▪ Generally support or oppose favoring union employers for business permits and city purchases;

▪ Would be more or less likely to support favoring union employers knowing that very few local businesses would be able to bid for city contractors or building contracts?

The questionnaire will be sent to only those who responded to a previous questionnaire sent by Councilmember Schroder, to a "high propensity" voter list, or to a combination of the two. On your current working draft, the only mention of Councilmember Schroder's official status as a council member is on the return address.

File No. A-02-116
Page No. 3

Councilmember Schroder intends to publish the questionnaire results in the local
newspaper and also post them on a web site currently being put together. There is no
intention of using the web site to promote Councilmember Schroder's candidacy for
mayor nor is there any ballot position being advocated.

ANALYSIS

A primary purpose of the Act is to ensure that receipts and expenditures in
election campaigns be fully and truthfully disclosed so that voters may be fully informed
and improper practices may be inhibited. (Section 81002(a).) In furtherance of this goal,
the Act requires a committee² to file periodic campaign statements disclosing its received
contributions and expenditures. (Sections 84200 - 84225.)

What Is a "Contribution"?

Section 82015(a) defines the term "contribution" as "a payment, a forgiveness of
a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment
except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from
the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." Regulation
18215(a) likewise establishes (in pertinent part) that "[a] contribution is any payment
made for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the
donor" and further clarifies that a payment is made for political purposes if it is "received
by or made at the behest of...[a] candidate." (Regulation 18215(a)(2)(A).) The term
"candidate" is defined in section 82007, as follows:

"'Candidate' means an individual who is listed on the ballot or who has
qualified to have write-in votes on his or her behalf counted by election
officials, for nomination for or election to any elective office, or who
receives a contribution or makes an expenditure or gives his or her consent
for any other person to receive a contribution or make an expenditure with
a view to bringing about his or her nomination or election to any elective
office, whether or not the specific elective office for which he or she will
seek nomination or election is known at the time the contribution is
received or the expenditure is made and whether or not he or she has
announced his or her candidacy or filed a declaration of candidacy at such
time. 'Candidate' also includes any officeholder who is the subject of a
recall election. An individual who becomes a candidate shall retain his or
her status as a candidate until such time as that status is terminated
pursuant to Section 84214. 'Candidate' does not include any person
within the meaning of Section 301(b) of the Federal Election Campaign

² "Committee" means any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly receives
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year; makes independent expenditures totaling $1,000
or more in a calendar year; or makes contributions totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar year to or at the
behest of candidates or committees. (Section 82013.)

³ Section 82044 defines a "payment" as a "payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or
other rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible."

File No. A-02-116
Page No. 4

Act of 1971." (Emphasis added.)

Whether in his status as a current council member or as a candidate for mayor,
Councilmember Schroder is considered to be a candidate under this definition, any
payment he receives is deemed to be for a political purpose and, thus, would be a
contribution, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment is not
for political purposes. The payment in question would be received by Councilmember
Schroder in connection with his status as an officeholder and could tangentially affect his
name recognition as a candidate for mayor. Moreover, the questionnaire will be sent to a
"high propensity" voter list and/or people who responded to an earlier questionnaire.
Therefore, we cannot say that the payment is not for political purposes.⁴ Accordingly, we
conclude that a payment received by Councilmember Schroder for this purpose would be
a contribution. Contributions to a candidate, under section 85201(c), must be deposited
in the candidate's bank account and reported pursuant to sections 84200 – 84217.

What Is an "Expenditure"?

Section 82025 defines expenditure as a "a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a
payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment, unless
it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes."
Regulation 18225(a) further explores what constitutes an expenditure as being "any
monetary or nonmonetary payment made for political purposes. A payment is made for
political purposes if it is . . . made by a candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding
circumstances that the payment was made for personal purposes unrelated to his or her
candidacy or status as an office holder." (Regulation 18225(a)(2)(A).) As we
determined above, Councilmember Schroder is a candidate and payments he receives to
produce and mail the questionnaire are for a political purpose. Based on the same logic,
expenditures Councilmember Schroder makes to produce and mail the questionnaire
would be for a political purpose and would likewise be expenditures. Therefore,
payments made by Councilmember Schroder in connection with mailing the
questionnaire are presumed to be expenditures. Expenditures made by a candidate must
be reported pursuant to sections 84200 – 84217.

You have asked what difference might exist in reporting if either the
questionnaires are mailed only to those who responded to the last questionnaires mailed
by Councilmember Schroder, or if a third party handles the mailing. In answer to the first
question, the Act contains no exemptions excluding payments for follow-up mailers from
being either contributions or expenditures. Regarding the second question, section
82015(b)(2)(B)(iii) states that a payment made at the behest⁵ of Councilmember Schroder
and made principally for legislative, governmental, or charitable purposes will not be

⁴ Your facts are also distinguishable, for example, from a situation where a water district commissioned a
poll without a council member's knowledge and the official did not participate in the conduct of the poll.
(Galstan Advice Letter No. I-92-120.)
⁵ Regulation 18225.7 explains that a payment or expenditure is "made at the behest of" a candidate when it
is "made under the control of or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with,
at the request or suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of."

File No. A-02-116
Page No. 5

considered a contribution, assuming the purpose is not "election related" (see section
82015(b)(2)(C)). If, however, during a calendar year, such payments equal or exceed
$5,000 in the aggregate from the same source then Councilmember Schroder must report
these payments within 30 days. Please note that the $5,000 reporting requirement relates
to payments directly made by a third party to vendors or persons other than
Councilmember Schroder or his agents in connection with this mailing. Payments made
directly to Councilmember Schroder or his agents will be campaign contributions as
discussed above. By contrast, moneys paid under the $5,000 reporting requirement are
not campaign contributions and would not be reported on Councilmember Schroder's
campaign reports. Instead, these payments must be reported to the City of Martinez and
shall contain the "name of payor, address of payor, amount of the payment, date or dates
the payment or payments were made, the name and address of the payee, a brief
description of the goods or services provided or purchased, if any, and a description of
the specific purpose or event for which the payment or payments were made." (Section
82015(b)(2)(B)(iii).)

In summary, payments received or made by Councilmember Schroder for the
purpose of mailing and preparing the questionnaire meet the test of being made for a
political purpose and must be reported as contributions or expenditures. Additionally,
Councilmember Schroder must report, as described above, any payments made at his
behest by another individual or entity for the mailing if the payments exceed $5,000 from
that source during a calendar year. Finally, you mentioned in your letter the possibility of
not including Councilmember Schroder's name in the return address of the mailing.
Please be aware that section 84305 states, in part, that "no candidate or committee shall
send a mass mailing" unless the name, street address, and city of the candidate or
committee are shown on the outside of each piece of mail...."

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, you may contact me toll-
free at 866/275-3772.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant

6 A "mass mailing" means over two hundred substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not include a
form letter or other mail which is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry."
(Section 82041.5.) (Emphasis added.)

QUESTION: l. Must Councilmember Schroder report contributions received and expenditures
made to mail out a questionnaire even though no ballot measure is being
advocated by the questionnaire?
2. Will it make a difference if Councilmember Schroder mails questionnaires only to
the 2,600 constituents who responded to a previous questionnaire sent out by the
council member?
3. If Councilmember Schroder stops production on the mail piece and gives
someone else the notes, drafts, and outlines for the purpose of sending a similar
questionnaire, will Councilmember Schroder incur any reporting obligations?

CONCLUSION: Commissionregulations appear at 2 Califomia Code of Regulations, sections
18009-18997. All references to regulations are to Tifle 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations.
:
1. Councilmember Schroder must report all contributions received and expenditures
made for political purposes including those made for the purpose of mailing out
the questionnaire.
2. This conclusion will not change even if Councilmember Schroder restricts the
mailing to the 2,600 constituents who responded to a previous questionnaire sent
out by Councilmember Schroder.
3. If another individual or entity mails the questionnaire at Councilmember
Schroder's behest, Councilmember Schroder will incur reporting obligations if the
entity makes a payment of $5,000 or more in a calend ar year to send the mailing.
